2: hō kai dekatēn apo pantōn emerisen Abraam, prōton men hermēneuomenos basileus dikaiosynēs, epeita de kai basileus Salēm, ho estin basileus eirēnēs — "and to him Abraham apportioned a tenth part of everything. He is first, by translation of his name, king of righteousness, and then he is also king of Salem, that is, king of peace" (basileus dikaiosynēs: king of righteousness — Melchizedek = melek [king] + tsedeq [righteousness]): the etymology-based typology: the name's meaning is part of the type's significance. Basileus Salēm = king of Shalom (peace) — two royal qualities (righteousness and peace) that characterize the Son's rule (cf. Psalms 72:1-3, 7; Isaiah 9:6-7).
3: apator, amētor, agenealogētos, mēte archēn hēmerōn mēte zōēs telos echōn — "without father, without mother, without genealogy, having neither beginning of days nor end of life" (apator, amētor, agenealogētos: without father, without mother, without genealogy — three privative adjectives): the crucial interpretive note: these predicates apply not to Melchizedek's biographical reality but to what the biblical text says about him. The Genesis narrative provides no genealogy, no birth, no death for Melchizedek — the text's silence is treated as typologically significant, not as historical information. Aphomoiōmenos de tō huiō tou theou (made like the Son of God) — Melchizedek is made-like the Son, not the reverse; the Son is the archetype, Melchizedek the type.
11: Ei men oun teleiōsis dia tēs Leuitikēs hierōsynēs ēn... ti eti chreia kata tēn taxin Melchisedek heteron anistasthai hierea — "now if perfection had been attainable through the Levitical priesthood... what further need would there have been for another priest to arise according to the order of Melchizedek?" (teleiōsis: perfection, completion — bringing to the goal; the subjunctive ēn in a contrary-to-fact conditional: perfection was not attained through the Levitical system): the argument from the existence of Psalms 110:4 — if the Levitical system sufficed, God would not have promised a different kind of priest. The existence of the promise proves the insufficiency of the existing system.
16: hos ou kata nomon entolēs sarkikēs gegonen alla kata dynamin zōēs akatalytou — "who has become a priest, not on the basis of a legal requirement concerning bodily descent, but by the power of an indestructible life" (entolēs sarkikēs: fleshly/bodily commandment — the Levitical requirement of physical descent from Aaron; dynamin zōēs akatalytou: the power of an indestructible life — akatalutos: unable to be dissolved, indestructible; zōē not merely biological life but the life of the resurrection-age): the Melchizedekian priesthood is grounded in a different ontological category — not hereditary biology but the indestructible life that overcame death.
22: kata tosouton kai kreittōnos diathēkēs gegonen engyos Iēsous — "this makes Jesus the guarantor of a better covenant" (engyos: guarantor, surety, pledge-person — the one who puts himself up as security for another's debt or obligation; a legal-commercial term): Jesus is the covenant's guarantor, not merely its mediator. Kreittōnos diathēkēs (of a better covenant) — the first use of the "better covenant" language that will be elaborated in Hebrews 8. Diathēkē can mean both "covenant" (Hebraic-LXX usage) and "will/testament" (Greek usage); Hebrews exploits the ambiguity in Hebrews 9:15-17.
25: hothen kai sōzein eis to panteles dynatai tous proserchomenous di' autou tō theō, pantote zōn eis to entynchanein hyper autōn — "consequently, he is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them" (eis to panteles: to the uttermost, completely, for all time — panteles combines the ideas of completeness and duration): the interceding high priest who always lives is the basis of the complete salvation — his permanence guarantees the completeness of the benefit. Entynchanein hyper autōn (to intercede for them — entynchanō: to meet/approach someone on behalf of another; the present infinitive indicates continuous intercession).
26: toioutos gar hēmin eprepen archiereus, hosios, akakos, amiantos, kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn, kai hypsēloteros tōn ouranōn genomenos — "for it was indeed fitting that we should have such a high priest, holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners, and exalted above the heavens" (hosios: devout, holy in relation to God; akakos: without evil, guileless; amiantos: undefiled, unstained; kechōrismenos apo tōn hamartōlōn: having been separated from sinners — the perfect participle indicates the permanent separation accomplished at the exaltation): the five adjectives form an ascending scale of purity culminating in the spatial exaltation above the heavens — the highest conceivable point of separation from the earthly-sinful realm.
1: houtos gar ho Melchisedek, basileus Salēm, hiereus tou theou tou hypsistou, ho synantēsas Abraam hypostrephonti apo tēs kopēs tōn basileōn kai eulogēsas auton — "this Melchizedek was king of Salem and priest of God Most High. He met Abraham returning from the defeat of the kings and blessed him" (Genesis 14:17-20 summarized): basileus Salēm, hiereus tou theou tou hypsistou — the dual title (king + priest) is the essential characteristic that makes Melchizedek typologically significant; no Levitical priest held both offices. Tou theou tou hypsistou (of God Most High — El Elyon): a divine title that transcends ethnic particularism — Abraham recognized the God whom Melchizedek served as the same God he himself worshipped (Genesis 14:22).
4: theōreite de pēlikos houtos, hō kai dekatēn Abraam edōken ek tōn akrothiniōn ho patriarchēs — "just think how great he was: the patriarch Abraham gave him a tenth of the plunder" (theōreite pēlikos houtos: consider how great this one is — pēlikos: how great in magnitude, how impressive; theōreite: present imperative — ongoing contemplation): the argument from Abraham's action: that ho patriarchēs (the patriarch — the founding father of the covenant people) gave a tithe to Melchizedek establishes Melchizedek's superiority over Abraham, and thus over all who descended from Abraham, including the Levitical priests. Akrothiniōn (top of the pile, best of the spoils): the tithe is of the prime portion, not the leftovers.
5: kai hoi men ek tōn huiōn Leui tēn hierōsynēn lambanontes entolēn echousin apodekatouto ton laon kata ton nomon, tout' estin tous adelphous autōn, kai per exelēlythōtas ek tēs osphyos Abraam — "now the law requires the descendants of Levi who become priests to collect a tenth from the people — that is, from their fellow Israelites — even though they also are descended from Abraham" (kata ton nomon: according to the law — the Levitical tithe is a legal requirement, not a voluntary act of homage): the irony noted: the Levitical priests collect a tithe from their own kinsmen-in-Abraham, yet Melchizedek — outside the Abrahamic line's priestly structure — received a tithe from Abraham himself. The tithe-giver is acknowledged as the lesser party.
6: ho de mē genealogoumenos ex autōn dedekatōken Abraam, kai ton echonta tas epangelias eulogēken — "this man, however, did not trace his descent from Levi, yet he collected a tenth from Abraham and blessed him who had the promises" (mē genealogoumenos ex autōn: not being traced in genealogy from them — the Levitical legitimacy rests on genealogical belonging; Melchizedek has no such genealogy and yet exercises superior priestly authority): dedekatōken (has tithed — perfect: the tithing stands as a permanent historical fact with ongoing typological force): ton echonta tas epangelias (the one who has the promises — Abraham, the promise-holder): Melchizedek blessed the one who held the covenant promises, establishing his superiority over the promise-holder himself.
7: chōris de pasēs antilogias to elatton hypo tou kreittonos eulogeitai — "and without doubt the lesser is blessed by the greater" (chōris pasēs antilogias: without any dispute/contradiction — antilogia: counter-word, objection): the logical axiom: the one who blesses stands in a position of superiority over the one blessed. Since Melchizedek blessed Abraham, Melchizedek is by definition the greater of the two. The axiom is presented as universally acknowledged — chōris pasēs antilogias closes off potential objections.
8: kai hōde men dekatous apothnēskontes anthrōpoi lambanousin, ekei de martyroumenos hoti zē — "in the one case, the tenth is collected by people who die; but in the other case, by him who is testified to be living" (apothnēskontes anthrōpoi: people who die — present participle: dying people collect tithes in the Levitical system; their mortality is an ongoing condition that limits the system's permanence): martyroumenos hoti zē (testified to be living — the passive martyroumenos: Scripture testifies of Melchizedek's living status by recording no death): the silence of the text about Melchizedek's death is treated as a positive testimony to his living — not biographical speculation but typological reading of textual silence.
9: kai hōs epos eipein, di' Abraam kai Leui ho dekatous lambanōn dedekatōtai — "one might even say that Levi, who collects the tenth, paid the tenth through Abraham" (hōs epos eipein: so to speak, if one may put it this way — a hedging expression used in classical Greek to introduce a bold or paradoxical statement without full assertion): the author acknowledges the argumentative leap with hōs epos eipein. Dedekatōtai (has been tithed — perfect passive): the argument rests on the concept of corporate solidarity — Levi's existence within Abraham's loins makes Levi participant in Abraham's actions. This is a Semitic concept of solidarity that the author applies to the genealogical-typological argument.
10: eti gar en tē osphyi tou patros ēn, hote synantēsen autō Melchisedek — "because when Melchizedek met Abraham, Levi was still in the body of his ancestor" (en tē osphyi tou patros: in the loins of the father/ancestor — osphys: loin, the reproductive seat in biblical anthropology): the argument assumes a corporate and physical understanding of generational solidarity: Levi was "present" in Abraham in the same sense that Isaac and Jacob were latent in Abraham's biological seed. This is the same logic by which Paul argues that all humanity participated in Adam's sin (Romans 5:12). The typological argument does not require the author to commit to Levi's personal awareness but to the representative structure of ancestry.
12: metatithemenēs gar tēs hierōsynēs ex anankēs kai nomou metathesis ginetai — "for when the priesthood is changed, the law must be changed also" (metatithemenēs: being changed, being transferred — present passive participle of metatithēmi: to transfer, to remove from one place/condition to another; the same root as ametatheton in Hebrews 6:17-18): the logical connection between priesthood and law: the Mosaic Torah is structurally organized around the Levitical priesthood (sacrificial law, purity law, the entire cultic apparatus). If the priesthood is superseded, the law itself is reorganized — not annulled but reorganized around the new priesthood of the Melchizedekian type.
13: eph' hon gar legetai tauta phylēs heterias metesche, aph' hēs oudeis proseschēken tō thysiastēriō — "he of whom these things are said belonged to a different tribe, and no one from that tribe has ever served at the altar" (phylēs heterias metesche: he shared in/belonged to a different tribe — heteros: another of a different kind; the tribe of Judah, not Levi): the factual observation that Jesus descended from Judah is the historical grounding for the argument. Tō thysiastēriō (the altar): the Levitical altar-service was tribe-specific — Judah had no standing there. The Son's priesthood must therefore be of a different order.
14: prodēlon gar hoti ex Iouda anatetalken ho kyrios hēmōn, eis hēn phylēn peri hiereon Mōysēs ouden elalēsen — "for it is clear that our Lord descended from Judah, and in regard to that tribe Moses said nothing about priests" (prodēlon: openly evident, perfectly clear — the descent from Judah is not disputed): anatetalken (has arisen/risen — perfect of anatellō: to rise as the sun rises, to spring up as a plant springs from the earth; the messianic "branch" language of Isaiah 11:1, Jeremiah 23:5, Zechariah 3:8 uses this root): the perfect tense indicates a permanent risen state. Peri hiereon Mōysēs ouden elalēsen (Moses said nothing about priests in regard to that tribe): the Torah's silence about Judah as a priestly tribe is itself evidence that the Son's priesthood operates outside Mosaic categories.
15: kai perissoteron eti katadēlon estin, ei kata tēn homoiotēta Melchisedek anistatatai hiereus heteros — "and this is even more clear if another priest like Melchizedek arises" (perissoteron eti katadēlon: more abundantly yet evident — intensive comparison; katadēlon: thoroughly evident, transparent): the double comparison (perissoteron + eti) emphasizes that the Melchizedek argument makes what was already clear even more transparent. Kata tēn homoiotēta Melchisedek (according to the likeness of Melchizedek): the "likeness" (homoiotēs) echoes Hebrews 4:15 — the same word used for Christ's likeness to human beings in testing; here the structural-priestly likeness to Melchizedek.
17: martyreitai gar hoti Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna kata tēn taxin Melchisedek — "for it is declared: You are a priest forever, in the order of Melchizedek" (Psalms 110:4 cited again): martyreitai (it is testified, it is declared — present passive: Scripture continues to testify, present tense): the Psalm testimony is not a past utterance but a continuing divine declaration. The citation anchors the entire priesthood-argument: the "forever" (eis ton aiōna) is the explicit scriptural warrant for the indestructible-life priesthood argument of v16.
18: athetēsis men gar ginetai proagousēs entolēs dia to autēs asthenēs kai anōpheles — "for on the one hand there is a setting aside of a former commandment because of its weakness and uselessness" (athetēsis: setting aside, annulment — from atheteō: to put away as if it did not exist, to declare null; a legal term): the language is precise about what has been set aside: not the entire Mosaic revelation but the specific entolē (commandment, regulation) concerning Levitical-bodily qualification. Asthenēs kai anōpheles (weak and useless): the Levitical system is not wrong or evil — it is asthenēs (weak, incapable of achieving its stated goal of perfection) and anōpheles (not profitable in the ultimate sense).
19: ouden gar eteleiōsen ho nomos, epeisagōgē de kreittōnos elpidos, di' hēs engizomen tō theō — "for the law made nothing perfect, and a better hope is introduced through which we draw near to God" (ouden eteleiōsen ho nomos: the law brought nothing to its goal — the telos-root: the law could not bring the worshiper to the telos of the relationship it promised): epeisagōgē (bringing in alongside/in addition — the prefix epi + eis: a supplementary introduction): the better hope is introduced not as a rejection of what preceded but as the fulfillment of what the law pointed toward. Engizomen tō theō (we draw near to God): the goal of the entire priestly system — access to God — is now achieved through the better hope.
20: kai kath' hoson ou chōris horkōmosia, hoi men gar chōris horkōmosias eisin hiereis gegonotes — "and it was not without an oath! Others became priests without any oath" (horkōmosia: oath-taking, the swearing of an oath — a compound noun appearing only in Hebrews in the NT): the contrast is between the Levitical ordination (governed by Torah regulation without a personal divine oath) and the Melchizedekian appointment (grounded in the divine oath of Psalms 110:4). The oath is the second element that makes Jesus's priesthood superior: not only is it of a different order and not only is it eternal, but it is divinely oath-secured.
21: ho de meta horkōmosias dia tou legontos pros auton, Ōmosen Kyrios kai ou metamelēthēsetai, Sy hiereus eis ton aiōna — "but he became a priest with an oath through the one who said to him: The Lord has sworn and will not change his mind: You are a priest forever" (Psalms 110:4): ou metamelēthēsetai (will not repent/change his mind — metamelomai: to have an after-care, to regret and reverse; the divine negative: God does not experience post-oath regret): the oath-formula emphasizes both the irrevocability of the appointment (sworn) and the immutability of the commitment (will not change his mind). The juxtaposition with the mortal-priest succession of v23 makes the contrast stark.
23: kai hoi men pleiones eisin gegonotes hiereis, dia to thanatō kōluesthai paramenein — "now there were many of those priests, since death prevented them from continuing in office" (kōluesthai paramenein: being prevented from continuing — kōlyō: to hinder, to obstruct; paramenō: to remain alongside, to continue in place): the plurality of Levitical priests is not a mark of the system's strength but of its structural limitation — each priest's death necessitated a successor, creating an inherently interrupted ministry. The many-priests problem is the death-problem: mortality prevents the continuity that effective priesthood requires.
24: ho de dia to menein auton eis ton aiōna aparabaton echei tēn hierōsynēn — "but because Jesus lives forever, he has a permanent priesthood" (aparabaton: not passing to another, permanent, inviolable — a NT hapax; from a-privative + parabainō: to step past/across): aparabatos means the priesthood cannot be crossed over to another, cannot be transgressed or bypassed; it is perpetual and non-transferable. The word combines the senses of permanence (it does not end) and exclusivity (it cannot be handed to another). Dia to menein auton eis ton aiōna (because of his abiding forever): the indestructible life (v16) is the ontological ground of the non-transferable priesthood.
27: hos ouk echei kath' hēmeran anankēn, hōsper hoi archiereis, proteron hyper tōn idiōn hamartiōn thysias anapherein, epeita tōn tou laou; touto gar epoiēsen ephapax heauton anenengkas — "unlike the other high priests, he does not need to offer sacrifices day after day, first for his own sins, and then for the sins of the people. He sacrificed for their sins once for all when he offered himself" (kath' hēmeran: according to the day, day by day — the dailiness of the Levitical system is its limitation; not even the annual high-priestly atonement suffices for the accumulation): ephapax (once for all) — the single completed offering is the answer to the daily necessity. Heauton anenengkas (having offered himself — reflexive: the high priest and the sacrifice are the same person, a structural impossibility in Levitical law): the self-offering resolves what no animal sacrifice could — the need for a priest without sin to offer a sacrifice sufficient once for all.
28: ho nomos gar anthrōpous kathistēsin archieras echontas astheneian, ho logos de tēs horkōmosias tēs meta ton nomon huion eis ton aiōna teteleiōmenon — "for the law appoints as high priests men in all their weakness; but the oath, which came after the law, appointed the Son, who has been made perfect forever" (kata ton nomon: according to the law, governed by the law; echontas astheneian: having weakness — the weakness is not moral infirmity alone but the structural weakness of mortality and its consequences): ho logos tēs horkōmosias (the word of the oath-taking) — the Psalms 110:4 oracle is a logos (a word with authority) expressed as an horkōmosia (oath). Teteleiōmenon eis ton aiōna (having been made perfect/complete forever — perfect passive participle): the Son's perfection through suffering (Hebrews 2:10, Hebrews 5:9) is now permanent; the word "forever" closes the argument of chapters 5-7.
